**STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP** Attorneys at Law

600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 Austin, Texas 78701

Telephone: (512) 555-7400 Facsimile: (512) 555-7401

July 10, 2023

VIA EMAIL AND CERTIFIED MAIL --- RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED

Jonathan Breckenridge, Esq. Wexford Hale LLP 300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500 Austin, Texas 78701

> Re: Arcadia Health Systems, LLC --- Demand and Threatened Litigation Against Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.; Master Software License and Services Agreement dated March 15, 2022

Dear Mr. Breckenridge:

We write on behalf of our client, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\"), in response to your letter dated June 26, 2023, directed to Meridian\'s General Counsel, Patricia M. Espinoza, in which you assert various claims on behalf of your client, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\"), arising from the Master Software License and Services Agreement executed on March 15, 2022 (the \"MSLSA\"), and the related Statement of Work #1 (\"SOW-1\"). Your letter demands payment of \$47.3 million in alleged damages and threatens litigation if Meridian does not accede to Arcadia\'s demands within thirty days.

We have conducted a thorough review of the MSLSA, SOW-1, all four executed Change Orders (CO-001 through CO-004), the contemporaneous project documentation, Meridian\'s internal records, and the relevant correspondence between the parties. Based on that review, Meridian categorically rejects each of Arcadia\'s asserted claims and demands. This letter sets forth the principal bases for Meridian\'s position. Nothing in this letter should be construed as a complete statement of Meridian\'s defenses, all of which are expressly reserved.

**[I. Arcadia\'s Breach of Contract Claim Is Without Merit]{.underline}**

Your letter alleges that Meridian breached the MSLSA by delivering defective software and failing to achieve timely implementation. These allegations fundamentally mischaracterize the contractual record.

**The Go-Live delay was attributable to Arcadia\'s own failures.** Meridian\'s project status report dated August 25, 2022 --- which was delivered to Arcadia contemporaneously and was never disputed --- identified three root causes for the delay beyond the September 1, 2022 target Go-Live date: (1) Arcadia\'s 45-day gap in designating a Project Manager after Kevin Liu\'s departure on April 28, 2022, in contravention of Section 4 of SOW-1; (2) Arcadia\'s failure to provide complete API specifications until July 20, 2022, which was 75 days past the milestone date established in the SOW-1 project schedule; and (3) critical errors in the data migration scripts prepared by Linden Park Consulting, LLC (\"Linden Park\"), the third-party systems integrator that Arcadia --- not Meridian --- independently retained. Section 3.2 of SOW-1 expressly provides that Arcadia \"shall be solely responsible for all data migration activities, including extraction, transformation, and loading of legacy data into NexusCore\" and that Meridian\'s role was limited to \"advisory consulting services.\" Your client cannot attribute implementation delays caused by its own systems integrator\'s deficient work product to Meridian.

**Arcadia accepted the software.** Under Section 5.3 of the MSLSA, Arcadia had 30 days following deployment of each module to conduct acceptance testing and deliver written notice of any material nonconformity. The NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module achieved Go-Live on January 15, 2023, and the acceptance testing window closed on February 14, 2023. Arcadia did not deliver any written notice of nonconformity within that period. Accordingly, acceptance was deemed granted under the express terms of Section 5.3. Dr. Okonkwo\'s email to Sandra Bledsoe dated March 8, 2023 --- which referenced only \"intermittent latency issues\" and \"occasional report generation errors\" --- was sent 22 days after the acceptance window closed and does not satisfy the contractual notice requirement.

**The limited warranty has expired and Meridian performed its cure obligations.** Section 9.1 of the MSLSA provides a limited warranty that the software will \"perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation\" for 90 days following Go-Live. That 90-day period expired on April 15, 2023. During the warranty period, Meridian\'s support team diligently addressed all reported issues. Of the 47 total support tickets Arcadia submitted between March and July 2023, 31 were resolved within the applicable SLA timeframes, 12 were traceable to data quality issues caused by the Linden Park migration (for which Meridian bore no contractual responsibility), and the remaining 4 involved software defects that Meridian patched through software updates released on May 2, 2023, and June 19, 2023. This performance was fully consistent with --- and indeed exceeded --- Meridian\'s obligations under the MSLSA.

**The damages sought are barred by the MSLSA\'s limitation of liability provisions.** Even if Arcadia could establish a breach (which it cannot), the MSLSA contains unambiguous limitations on recoverable damages. Section 8.1 caps either party\'s aggregate liability at \"the total fees paid or payable by Licensee during the twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the event giving rise to such liability.\" Section 8.2 expressly excludes liability for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, or exemplary damages, including lost profits, goodwill, and reputational harm. Arcadia\'s \$47.3 million demand --- comprising \$18.4 million in lost profits, \$6.7 million in increased operating costs, and \$5.0 million in purported reputational harm --- is overwhelmingly composed of consequential damages that are categorically excluded by the parties\' agreement.

**[II. Arcadia\'s Fraud and Negligent Misrepresentation Claims Fail as a Matter of Law]{.underline}**

Your letter alleges that Meridian, through its Account Executive James Poletti, made fraudulent representations during the pre-sale process regarding NexusCore\'s capabilities and integration functionality. These claims are legally deficient for multiple independent reasons.

**The integration clause bars reliance on extra-contractual representations.** Section 12.1 of the MSLSA provides that the Agreement \"constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written.\" Under Delaware law, which governs the MSLSA pursuant to Section 12.7, a merger clause of this breadth is enforceable and precludes claims based on alleged pre-contractual representations that were not incorporated into the written agreement. *See Abry Partners V, L.P. v. F&W Acquisition LLC*, 891 A.2d 1032, 1059 (Del. Ch. 2006).

**The alleged misrepresentations are non-actionable puffery or forward-looking statements.** The sales presentation materials referenced general marketing language --- \"industry-leading performance\" and \"30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency\" --- which constitutes classic puffery that is not actionable as fraud under either Delaware or Texas law. Moreover, Meridian\'s December 9, 2021 written proposal contained express disclaimers that \"\[e\]stimated timelines and performance metrics are provided for planning purposes only and do not constitute guarantees.\"

**Arcadia cannot establish justifiable reliance.** Arcadia is a sophisticated commercial entity with approximately \$62 million in annual revenue, represented by experienced outside counsel during the negotiation of the MSLSA. Arcadia\'s own CTO, Martin Schreiber, conducted independent due diligence --- including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients and a two-week proof-of-concept trial --- before contract execution. Mr. Schreiber\'s own internal memorandum acknowledged that \"\[i\]ntegration complexity is manageable but will require a skilled SI partner.\" This conclusively demonstrates that Arcadia made its purchasing decision based on its own independent investigation, not on any alleged representations by Mr. Poletti. Any claim of justifiable reliance on general sales statements in the face of this independent diligence and a comprehensive integration clause fails as a matter of law.

**[III. Arcadia\'s Unjust Enrichment Claim Is Precluded by the MSLSA]{.underline}**

Arcadia\'s unjust enrichment claim is barred because a valid, enforceable contract --- the MSLSA --- governs the subject matter of the dispute. Under both Delaware and Texas law, an unjust enrichment claim cannot be maintained where an express contract covers the same subject matter. *See Nemec v. Shrader*, 991 A.2d 1120, 1130 (Del. 2010); *Fortis Benefits v. Cantu*, 234 S.W.3d 642, 649 (Tex. 2007). Arcadia does not and cannot allege that the MSLSA is void or unenforceable. Accordingly, this claim must be abandoned.

**[IV. Arcadia\'s DTPA Claim Is Barred by the Section 17.49(f) Exemption and the Consumer Status Requirement]{.underline}**

Your letter asserts a claim under the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices --- Consumer Protection Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Sections 17.41--17.63 (the \"DTPA\"), and demands \$2.5 million in treble damages. This claim is without foundation.

**The DTPA exemption for large transactions applies.** Section 17.49(f) of the Texas Business and Commerce Code exempts from DTPA coverage any \"cause of action arising from a transaction, a project, or a set of transactions relating to the same project, involving total consideration by the consumer of more than \$500,000\" unless the consumer is an individual. Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company, not an individual. The total consideration under the MSLSA alone was \$14,700,000, and the total consideration including the four Change Orders was \$17,050,000 --- more than thirty-four times the statutory threshold. The exemption plainly applies, and Arcadia\'s DTPA claim is barred.

We note that Section 17.49(f) also provides an exemption where the defendant\'s \"assets at the time of the act or practice complained of\" exceed \$25 million. To preserve our client\'s rights and fully evaluate all applicable exemptions, **we hereby request that Arcadia produce documentation of its total assets as of March 15, 2022, including but not limited to audited or reviewed financial statements, balance sheets, and tax returns**. To the extent Arcadia\'s total assets exceeded \$25 million at the relevant time --- which we believe likely given Arcadia\'s reported annual revenues of approximately \$62 million and its operations spanning 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics --- an additional basis for the Section 17.49(f) exemption exists.

**Arcadia does not qualify as a \"consumer\" under the DTPA.** Even if the Section 17.49(f) exemption were somehow inapplicable, the DTPA protects only a \"consumer,\" defined under Section 17.45(4) as \"an individual, partnership, corporation, this state, or a subdivision or agency of this state who seeks or acquires by purchase or lease, any goods or services.\" Texas courts have consistently held that to qualify as a \"consumer,\" the claimant must have sought or acquired goods or services, and the transaction must have a consumer, rather than purely commercial, character. The MSLSA is a sophisticated enterprise software licensing agreement negotiated at arm\'s length between two commercial entities, each represented by counsel, with a total contract value of \$14.7 million. This is not the type of consumer transaction the DTPA was enacted to protect. *See Riverside Nat\'l Bank v. Lewis*, 603 S.W.2d 169, 174--75 (Tex. 1980) (analyzing consumer status under the DTPA).

**[V. Reservation of Rights]{.underline}**

Meridian reserves all rights, remedies, claims, and defenses available to it under the MSLSA, at law, and in equity, including but not limited to all affirmative defenses, rights under the contractual limitation of liability and consequential damages waiver provisions, and any counterclaims arising from Arcadia\'s own material breaches of the MSLSA and SOW-1. Nothing in this letter constitutes a waiver of any such right or defense, nor an admission of any fact or legal theory.

Meridian encourages Arcadia to carefully reconsider the merits of its threatened claims in light of the foregoing. Should Arcadia wish to discuss a practical resolution of any outstanding operational issues with the NexusCore platform, Meridian remains willing to engage in good-faith discussions regarding ongoing support, subject to the terms of the MSLSA. However, Meridian will not entertain demands for payment grounded in meritless legal theories.

If Arcadia chooses to pursue litigation notwithstanding this response, Meridian will defend vigorously and will seek recovery of its attorneys\' fees and costs to the fullest extent permitted by law and the MSLSA.

Please direct all further correspondence regarding this matter to the undersigned.

Very truly yours,

STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Margaret \"Meg\" Calloway Lead Partner

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

David Arsenault Senior Associate

cc: Patricia M. Espinoza, General Counsel, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.

result:
**ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC** **INTERNAL MEMORANDUM --- CONFIDENTIAL**

**TO:** Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, Managing Member / CEO **FROM:** Martin Schreiber, Chief Technology Officer **DATE:** January 19, 2022 **RE:** NexusCore Platform --- Due Diligence Findings and Recommendation

**[1. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

Over the past several weeks, I have conducted an independent technical and operational due diligence review of Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\'s NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module as a potential replacement for our legacy HealthBridge 4.0 platform. This review included reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients, a two-week proof-of-concept (\"POC\") trial conducted in our San Antonio test environment, and an assessment of NexusCore\'s architecture, API framework, and reporting capabilities.

My overall assessment is positive. **NexusCore appears well-suited to our needs. Integration complexity is manageable but will require a skilled SI partner.** The platform\'s modular architecture, cloud-native design, and healthcare-specific analytics tooling represent a meaningful upgrade from HealthBridge 4.0. However, there are integration considerations --- particularly around data migration and EHR connectivity across our 23 hospitals and 140+ clinic sites --- that we must plan for carefully. I recommend we proceed to contract negotiations with Meridian, subject to the conditions outlined in Section 6 below.

**[2. Reference Calls]{.underline}**

I conducted reference calls with technology leadership at two existing Meridian healthcare clients. Both organizations agreed to speak candidly on the condition that their feedback remain confidential. Below is a summary of each conversation.

**2.1 San Marcos Regional Health**

**Contact:** David Faulkner, Vice President of Information Systems **Date of Call:** January 6, 2022 **Duration:** Approximately 45 minutes

San Marcos Regional Health (\"SMRH\") is a mid-sized regional health system operating seven hospitals and approximately 60 affiliated clinics in the greater San Marcos area. SMRH deployed NexusCore\'s Healthcare Analytics Module in Q2 2021 and has been live on the platform for approximately seven months at the time of our call.

Mr. Faulkner reported that the implementation took approximately five months from kickoff to go-live, which he described as \"on the longer side of what Meridian originally estimated, but within the range we expected given our environment.\" He noted that the primary driver of timeline extension was SMRH\'s own internal resource constraints during the configuration phase, not deficiencies on Meridian\'s side. He specifically praised Meridian\'s project management team and stated that Meridian\'s technical consultants were \"responsive and knowledgeable.\"

Regarding software performance, Faulkner indicated that NexusCore\'s reporting engine is \"significantly faster\" than their prior analytics platform and that the healthcare-specific data models reduced the amount of custom configuration they needed. He noted occasional latency during peak usage hours in the first two months post-go-live, but said Meridian addressed the issue through a server-side optimization within approximately three weeks of their support ticket.

On the subject of data migration, Faulkner offered an important caution. SMRH initially attempted to handle data migration internally and encountered \"serious data integrity issues\" that delayed their go-live by approximately three weeks. They ultimately brought in an outside systems integrator to remediate the migration scripts and complete the data load. Faulkner stated: \"If I had to do it over again, I would have engaged an experienced SI partner for the migration from day one. That is not Meridian\'s core competency, and they are upfront about that.\"

**Overall assessment from SMRH: Positive.** Faulkner said he would recommend NexusCore and stated that SMRH is evaluating an expansion of its Meridian deployment to include additional modules.

**2.2 Coastal Bend Medical Group**

**Contact:** Anita Suresh, Chief Information Officer **Date of Call:** January 10, 2022 **Duration:** Approximately 35 minutes

Coastal Bend Medical Group (\"CBMG\") is a large multi-specialty physician group operating 42 clinic locations across the Corpus Christi metropolitan area. CBMG deployed NexusCore in Q4 2020 and has been operating on the platform for over a year.

Ms. Suresh was broadly positive about the NexusCore platform. She highlighted the analytics dashboards as a \"game-changer\" for CBMG\'s value-based care reporting and noted that NexusCore\'s standard API connectors worked well with their primary EHR system. However, she noted that CBMG\'s environment involved only a single EHR platform, and she cautioned that organizations running multiple EHR systems would face \"a different level of integration complexity.\" She recommended building ample time into the project plan for API configuration and testing in multi-EHR environments.

Suresh also emphasized the importance of data quality in the migration process. She stated that CBMG invested approximately six weeks in data cleansing and validation prior to migration and that this investment \"paid for itself many times over\" by avoiding post-go-live data quality issues. She observed that organizations that underinvest in data preparation tend to attribute resulting data quality problems to the new platform rather than to the underlying data.

When asked about Meridian\'s support organization, Suresh rated it \"above average\" and noted that their assigned customer success manager was proactive in scheduling quarterly business reviews. She mentioned one frustration --- that Meridian\'s standard reporting templates required \"more customization than the sales team led us to believe\" --- but characterized this as a \"manageable issue\" that was resolved through their implementation services engagement.

**Overall assessment from CBMG: Positive with caveats.** Suresh recommended NexusCore but stressed the need for realistic expectations around integration timelines in complex environments.

**[3. Proof-of-Concept Trial]{.underline}**

**3.1 Trial Parameters**

We conducted a two-week proof-of-concept trial of the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module from January 4 through January 18, 2022. Meridian provisioned a dedicated cloud instance and loaded a de-identified sample dataset that we provided, representing approximately 180,000 patient records drawn from three of our hospital sites. The POC environment included NexusCore\'s standard healthcare analytics dashboards, reporting engine, and API sandbox.

Our evaluation team consisted of myself, Kevin Liu (Project Manager), two senior data engineers from our analytics team, and one clinical informatics specialist.

**3.2 Functional Evaluation**

The POC trial confirmed that NexusCore\'s core analytics capabilities are robust and represent a substantial improvement over HealthBridge 4.0. Specific findings include:

> • **Reporting Performance:** Report generation times averaged 3.2 seconds for standard dashboards and 11.7 seconds for complex ad hoc queries against the 180,000-record dataset. By comparison, equivalent reports in HealthBridge 4.0 average 14--18 seconds and 45+ seconds, respectively. This performance improvement is meaningful for our operational users.
>
> • **Healthcare Data Models:** NexusCore\'s pre-built healthcare data models (including FHIR-aligned structures, ICD-10 mapping tables, and CMS quality measure templates) are comprehensive and well-documented. Our clinical informatics specialist confirmed that the models align with our reporting requirements for value-based care contracts.
>
> • **User Interface:** The dashboard interface is modern, intuitive, and configurable. Our team required minimal training to navigate the POC environment. The drag-and-drop report builder is a significant upgrade from HealthBridge 4.0\'s manual query interface.
>
> • **API Framework:** We tested NexusCore\'s standard API connectors against a simulated EHR endpoint. The connectors functioned as documented for standard HL7 FHIR R4 transactions. However, our team noted that the standard connectors do not natively support several legacy data exchange formats that some of our older clinic-based EHR systems still use. Meridian\'s pre-sales engineer, during a follow-up technical call, confirmed that custom API connectors would be required for these legacy systems and that this would need to be scoped as part of the implementation services engagement.

**3.3 Identified Risks and Limitations**

The POC trial also revealed several areas of concern that must be addressed in implementation planning:

1\. **Multi-EHR Integration Complexity:** Our environment includes five distinct EHR platforms across our 23 hospitals and 140+ clinics. The POC tested connectivity with only one EHR system. Integrating with all five platforms --- particularly the two legacy systems still running non-FHIR-compliant interfaces --- will be a significant workstream. This aligns with Ms. Suresh\'s caution from the CBMG reference call. I estimate this will require dedicated API development and extensive testing cycles.

2\. **Data Migration Scale:** Our HealthBridge 4.0 environment contains approximately 12.8 million patient records, 340 million clinical data points, and over seven years of historical reporting data. The POC used only a 180,000-record sample. Migrating the full production dataset will be an order-of-magnitude more complex undertaking. Both reference clients emphasized that data migration was the single most challenging aspect of their NexusCore deployments. Based on what I have learned, **I strongly recommend that we engage an experienced, independent systems integration firm to manage the data migration workstream.** This should not be treated as a task our internal team handles on the side.

3\. **Customization Requirements:** While NexusCore\'s standard reporting templates cover approximately 70--75% of our current reporting needs, the remaining 25--30% will require custom report development. Some of our contractual reporting obligations with managed care payors involve non-standard data formats and calculations that are specific to our organization. This customization work should be scoped explicitly in any Statement of Work.

4\. **Change Management:** The shift from HealthBridge 4.0 to NexusCore will require significant change management across our clinical and operational staff. NexusCore\'s interface paradigm is fundamentally different from HealthBridge 4.0, and we should budget for an extended training and adoption period. I recommend we plan for at least 12--16 weeks of parallel operation during the transition.

**[4. Vendor Financial and Operational Assessment]{.underline}**

Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: MCSI) with reported annual revenue of approximately \$380 million. I reviewed their most recent publicly available financial filings and confirmed that the company appears financially stable, with consistent revenue growth and positive operating cash flow. Their customer base includes several prominent healthcare organizations, and the company has invested in dedicated healthcare vertical product development over the past three years.

I see no material vendor viability risk at this time.

**[5. Comparison to Alternatives]{.underline}**

For completeness, I note that we also evaluated two alternative platforms earlier in our vendor selection process: MedAnalytica Cloud (from Hargrove Technologies) and InsightRx Enterprise (from Cascade Data Systems). NexusCore outperformed both alternatives in our weighted scoring matrix across the categories of healthcare-specific functionality, reporting speed, API extensibility, and total cost of ownership over a three-year period. The primary advantage NexusCore holds over the alternatives is its pre-built healthcare data models, which significantly reduce configuration time and ongoing maintenance burden.

**[6. Recommendations and Conditions for Proceeding]{.underline}**

Based on my due diligence review, I recommend that we proceed to contract negotiations with Meridian Cloud Solutions for the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module, subject to the following conditions:

1\. **Retain a Qualified Systems Integrator.** We must engage a reputable, experienced systems integration partner to manage the data migration workstream. This is not optional. The reference calls and POC trial both confirmed that data migration is the highest-risk element of this project, and Meridian itself does not position data migration as a service it provides beyond advisory consulting. I recommend we begin evaluating SI candidates immediately so that we can have a partner identified and under contract by the time implementation begins.

2\. **Negotiate Detailed Statement of Work.** The SOW must clearly delineate responsibilities between Meridian, our internal team, and the SI partner. In particular, API integration responsibilities, acceptance testing criteria, and data migration accountability must be explicitly allocated. Ambiguity here will create risk.

3\. **Allocate Adequate Internal Resources.** We will need to designate a dedicated full-time Project Manager to serve as our single point of contact with Meridian throughout the implementation. This person must have decision-making authority and deep knowledge of our systems environment. I recommend Kevin Liu for this role. We will also need to commit at least two senior data engineers and one clinical informatics specialist to the project on a half-time basis during the implementation phase.

4\. **Budget for Contingency.** Based on the reference calls and my experience with enterprise software deployments of this scale, I recommend building a 15--20% cost contingency into our implementation budget to cover scope adjustments, unforeseen integration challenges, and additional customization needs. Enterprise ERP deployments of this nature rarely come in exactly at initial estimates.

5\. **Engage Outside Counsel for Contract Review.** Given the contract value and the complexity of the implementation, I recommend that we engage outside counsel to review and negotiate the MSLSA. Particular attention should be given to limitation of liability provisions, warranty terms, acceptance testing procedures, and the allocation of responsibility between licensor and licensee.

**[7. Conclusion]{.underline}**

NexusCore is a strong platform that addresses our core requirements and represents a generational improvement over HealthBridge 4.0. The risks I have identified --- multi-EHR integration complexity, data migration scale, and customization needs --- are manageable with proper planning, adequate resourcing, and a skilled systems integration partner. These are execution risks, not platform risks. I am confident that with the right implementation approach, NexusCore can deliver meaningful value to Arcadia.

I am available to discuss these findings at your convenience and recommend we schedule a leadership review meeting within the next two weeks to make a formal go/no-go decision.

Martin Schreiber Chief Technology Officer Arcadia Health Systems, LLC 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210 San Antonio, TX 78229 mschreiber@arcadiahealth.com

*This memorandum is confidential and intended solely for the internal use of Arcadia Health Systems, LLC leadership. Distribution outside the organization is prohibited without the prior written consent of the CTO.*

**[2. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

Arcadia Health Systems, LLC is a growing regional healthcare IT services provider currently operating electronic health record integration and analytics platforms for 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics across Texas and Louisiana. With annual revenue of approximately \$62 million, Arcadia has established itself as a critical technology partner for mid-market healthcare organizations in the Gulf Coast region.

Arcadia has identified an urgent need to replace its legacy analytics platform, HealthBridge 4.0, which was originally deployed in 2016 and has reached the limits of its scalability, reporting flexibility, and ability to support modern data interoperability standards. Arcadia\'s leadership team has communicated the following strategic priorities:

> • **Modernization of Analytics Infrastructure.** Arcadia requires a cloud-native analytics platform capable of processing large-scale clinical and operational data sets in near real-time, replacing the batch-processing architecture of HealthBridge 4.0.
>
> • **Multi-Facility Scalability.** The platform must support Arcadia\'s current network of 23 hospitals and 140+ clinics and accommodate anticipated growth over the next five years.
>
> • **EHR Interoperability.** The solution must integrate with the diverse array of EHR systems deployed across Arcadia\'s client facilities, including platforms from multiple vendors, through standards-based API connectivity.
>
> • **Regulatory Compliance.** All data handling, storage, and reporting must comply with HIPAA, HITECH, and applicable state privacy regulations.
>
> • **Operational Efficiency.** Arcadia seeks measurable improvements in reporting cycle times, data accuracy, and staff productivity. Internal targets include a 30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency relative to HealthBridge 4.0.

**Meridian\'s Proposed Solution.** Meridian proposes the deployment of the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module, a component of Meridian\'s flagship NexusCore cloud ERP platform. NexusCore is a modular, cloud-native platform purpose-built for enterprise-scale data analytics and operational intelligence. The Healthcare Analytics Module provides specialized functionality for clinical data aggregation, population health analytics, financial performance reporting, and regulatory compliance dashboarding tailored to the healthcare sector.

**Key Benefits.** NexusCore delivers industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics. Our clients typically see 30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency, reduced manual data reconciliation effort, and enhanced visibility into clinical and operational performance metrics. NexusCore\'s open API framework is designed to integrate with major EHR platforms through our standard API framework, subject to proper configuration and the availability of compliant data interfaces from the EHR vendors.

**Investment Overview.** The total proposed contract value is \$14,700,000, encompassing a three-year software license, Meridian-led implementation services, first-year maintenance and support, comprehensive training, and data migration advisory consulting. Additional detail on pricing is provided in Section 8 of this proposal.

**Implementation Timeline.** Meridian proposes a phased implementation commencing upon contract execution, with a target Go-Live date approximately six months following the project kickoff, subject to the assumptions and dependencies set forth in Section 9.

**[4. Understanding Arcadia\'s Requirements]{.underline}**

Based on Meridian\'s discovery sessions with Arcadia\'s leadership and technical teams on October 12, 2021, and November 18, 2021, Meridian understands Arcadia\'s requirements as follows:

**4.1 Current State Assessment**

Arcadia currently relies on HealthBridge 4.0, a proprietary analytics platform deployed in 2016. Key limitations of the current environment include:

> • **Scalability Constraints.** HealthBridge 4.0 was architected for a maximum of 15 concurrent hospital connections. Arcadia\'s current footprint of 23 hospitals exceeds this design threshold, resulting in degraded query performance and periodic system timeouts during peak reporting periods.
>
> • **Batch Processing Architecture.** HealthBridge 4.0 relies on overnight batch processing for data ingestion, resulting in a minimum 24-hour data latency for operational and clinical reports.
>
> • **Limited API Support.** HealthBridge 4.0 supports only proprietary data exchange formats and does not natively support HL7 FHIR, CDA, or modern REST API standards, requiring costly custom middleware for each EHR connection.
>
> • **Aging Codebase.** The HealthBridge 4.0 platform is built on a technology stack that is approaching end-of-life for several critical components, creating increasing maintenance burden and security risk.

**4.2 Desired Future State**

Arcadia has articulated the following requirements for a replacement analytics platform:

1\. Cloud-native architecture with horizontal scalability to support 50+ hospital connections and 500+ clinic endpoints.

2\. Near real-time data ingestion with latency of no more than 15 minutes for priority data feeds.

3\. Standards-based API connectivity (HL7 FHIR R4, REST, SOAP) for EHR integration.

4\. Pre-built healthcare analytics content including population health dashboards, quality measure reporting (HEDIS, CMS Star Ratings), and financial performance analysis.

5\. Role-based access control and comprehensive audit logging compliant with HIPAA and HITECH requirements.

6\. Self-service reporting capabilities allowing Arcadia\'s end users to build custom reports without developer intervention.

7\. Comprehensive training program for Arcadia\'s technical and business user communities.

8\. Data migration support --- advisory consulting and technical guidance for the transition from HealthBridge 4.0 to the new platform.

**4.3 Scope Boundaries**

Based on discussions during the November 18, 2021, demonstration, the parties have agreed on the following scope boundaries for this proposal:

> • **In Scope:** NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module; system configuration; user interface customization; report template development; API integration with Arcadia\'s existing EHR systems; training; data migration advisory consulting.
>
> • **Out of Scope:** Physical data migration execution (extraction, transformation, and loading of legacy data); Arcadia\'s internal network infrastructure upgrades; EHR vendor-side configuration changes; third-party systems integration services.

Arcadia has indicated its intent to retain a qualified third-party systems integrator for data migration and related activities. Meridian recommends that Arcadia engage such a partner early in the implementation process to ensure alignment with the overall project timeline.

**[5. Proposed Solution: NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module]{.underline}**

**5.1 Platform Overview**

NexusCore is a multi-tenant, cloud-native enterprise platform built on a microservices architecture. The Healthcare Analytics Module is one of twelve available NexusCore modules, purpose-built for healthcare data aggregation, transformation, and analytics. Key capabilities include:

> • **Data Ingestion Engine.** Supports concurrent ingestion of structured and semi-structured data from multiple sources, including EHR systems, claims data, pharmacy records, lab results, and third-party data feeds. The engine supports HL7 FHIR R4, HL7 v2.x, CDA, X12 (837/835), and custom flat-file formats.
>
> • **Analytics and Reporting.** Pre-built analytics content library with over 200 healthcare-specific report templates, dashboards, and scorecards. Content areas include clinical quality, population health, financial performance, utilization management, and regulatory compliance. Self-service report builder with drag-and-drop functionality for business users.
>
> • **Clinical Data Repository.** Centralized, normalized data repository optimized for healthcare data models (including OMOP CDM compatibility). Supports longitudinal patient records across multiple facilities and EHR systems.
>
> • **Population Health Management.** Risk stratification algorithms, care gap identification, chronic disease management analytics, and predictive modeling capabilities.
>
> • **Regulatory and Compliance Reporting.** Pre-configured templates for HEDIS, CMS Star Ratings, MIPS/APM quality reporting, and state-specific regulatory submissions.
>
> • **Security and Access Control.** Role-based and attribute-based access control, patient-level data segmentation, comprehensive audit logging, and break-the-glass emergency access protocols. Full HIPAA and HITECH compliance.

**5.2 Modular Architecture**

NexusCore\'s modular architecture allows Arcadia to deploy the Healthcare Analytics Module as a standalone solution while retaining the option to add additional NexusCore modules (e.g., Revenue Cycle Management, Supply Chain, HR/Workforce Management) in the future without re-platforming.

**5.3 Performance and Scalability**

The NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module is designed to support enterprise-scale deployments:

> • **Concurrent Users:** Up to 5,000 named users per instance.
>
> • **Data Volume:** Tested to 50 TB of active analytical data per tenant.
>
> • **Query Performance:** Sub-10-second response time for standard dashboard queries on datasets up to 10 TB (based on Meridian\'s standard benchmark environment; actual performance may vary depending on query complexity, data volume, and network conditions).
>
> • **Uptime SLA:** 99.9% platform availability, measured monthly, excluding scheduled maintenance windows.

**5.4 Integration Capabilities**

NexusCore provides a comprehensive API framework for integration with third-party systems:

> • **Standard Connectors.** Pre-built connectors for 18 major EHR platforms, including those most commonly deployed in the Texas and Louisiana healthcare markets. Meridian\'s standard API framework is designed to integrate with major EHR platforms, subject to proper configuration and the provision of compliant data interfaces from the EHR vendors.
>
> • **Custom Connector Toolkit.** For EHR systems not covered by pre-built connectors, Meridian provides a Custom Connector Toolkit enabling development of purpose-built interfaces. Custom connector development is included within the scope of Meridian\'s proposed implementation services (see Section 7).
>
> • **Data Specifications Requirement.** Successful API integration requires that the Licensee provide complete, accurate API specifications and access to test environments for each EHR system to be connected. Meridian\'s responsibility is limited to building and configuring API connectors in accordance with the specifications provided.

**[6. Technical Architecture and Integration Approach]{.underline}**

**6.1 Deployment Model**

NexusCore will be deployed as a dedicated tenant within Meridian\'s cloud infrastructure, hosted in Meridian\'s primary data center in Dallas, Texas, with failover capability to a secondary data center in Atlanta, Georgia. Arcadia\'s data will be logically isolated from other tenants through Meridian\'s multi-tenant isolation framework.

**6.2 Integration Architecture**

The proposed integration architecture for Arcadia consists of the following components:

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Layer**               **Component**                     **Description**
  ----------------------- --------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------
  Ingestion               NexusCore Data Ingestion Engine   Receives data from EHR systems via API connectors

  Transformation          NexusCore ETL Pipeline            Normalizes, cleanses, and transforms ingested data

  Storage                 Clinical Data Repository          Centralized, OMOP-compatible data store

  Analytics               NexusCore Analytics Engine        Executes queries, aggregations, and predictive models

  Presentation            NexusCore Dashboard Framework     Delivers reports, dashboards, and scorecards to end users

  Security                NexusCore Security Layer          Manages authentication, authorization, encryption, and audit
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**6.3 Data Migration Approach**

As noted in Section 4.3, physical data migration --- the extraction, transformation, and loading of data from HealthBridge 4.0 into NexusCore --- is outside the scope of Meridian\'s proposed services. Meridian recommends that Arcadia retain a qualified third-party systems integrator experienced in healthcare data migration to perform these activities.

Meridian will provide data migration advisory consulting services, which include:

> • Detailed documentation of NexusCore data models, schemas, and import specifications
>
> • Technical guidance on data mapping from HealthBridge 4.0 data structures to NexusCore data models
>
> • Validation rules and quality checks for migrated data
>
> • Consultation sessions with Arcadia\'s selected systems integrator (up to 120 hours of advisory time)
>
> • Review and validation of sample data loads during the migration process

**Arcadia shall be solely responsible for all data migration activities, including the selection and management of any third-party systems integrator, the extraction of data from HealthBridge 4.0, the transformation of data to conform to NexusCore specifications, and the loading of data into the NexusCore platform. Meridian shall have no responsibility for the accuracy, completeness, or integrity of migrated data.**

**[7. Implementation Methodology and Timeline]{.underline}**

**7.1 Implementation Methodology**

Meridian employs a structured, phased implementation methodology for NexusCore deployments, consisting of five phases:

**Phase 1 --- Discovery and Planning (Weeks 1-4)**

> • Detailed requirements workshops with Arcadia\'s business and technical stakeholders
>
> • System architecture design and environment provisioning
>
> • Project plan development, including milestone schedule and resource allocation
>
> • Identification and documentation of all EHR integration requirements
>
> • Arcadia to designate a Project Manager with decision-making authority

**Phase 2 --- Configuration and Customization (Weeks 5-12)**

> • System configuration based on requirements gathered in Phase 1
>
> • User interface customization to align with Arcadia\'s branding and workflow requirements
>
> • Development of custom report templates and dashboards
>
> • API connector development and unit testing
>
> • Arcadia to provide complete API specifications and access to EHR test environments

**Phase 3 --- Integration and Data Validation (Weeks 13-18)**

> • End-to-end API integration testing with Arcadia\'s EHR systems
>
> • Data flow validation and reconciliation
>
> • Coordination with Arcadia\'s systems integrator on data migration activities (advisory capacity)
>
> • Security and compliance validation, including penetration testing and HIPAA compliance review

**Phase 4 --- User Acceptance Testing and Training (Weeks 19-22)**

> • Deployment of the platform to Arcadia\'s UAT environment
>
> • Structured User Acceptance Testing per mutually agreed test scripts
>
> • Comprehensive training program (see Section 7.3)
>
> • Remediation of defects identified during UAT

**Phase 5 --- Go-Live and Stabilization (Weeks 23-26)**

> • Production deployment
>
> • Go-Live support with dedicated Meridian on-site team
>
> • 30-day post-Go-Live hypercare period
>
> • Transition to standard maintenance and support

**[9. Assumptions and Dependencies]{.underline}**

The proposed implementation timeline, pricing, and scope are contingent upon the following assumptions and dependencies. Deviation from these assumptions may result in schedule adjustments, scope changes, and/or pricing modifications, to be addressed through the Change Order process.

**9.1 Arcadia Obligations and Dependencies**

1\. **Project Manager.** Arcadia shall designate a Project Manager with appropriate decision-making authority no later than five (5) business days following contract execution. The Project Manager shall serve as the primary point of contact for all implementation-related matters and shall be authorized to approve deliverables, milestone completions, and day-to-day project decisions on Arcadia\'s behalf. Failure to designate or maintain a Project Manager may result in project delays attributable to Arcadia.

2\. **Subject Matter Experts.** Arcadia shall make subject matter experts available for requirements gathering workshops, data validation sessions, and UAT activities as reasonably requested by Meridian\'s implementation team. Meridian estimates a requirement of 4-6 Arcadia SMEs during Phase 1 and 2-3 SMEs during Phases 2 through 5.

3\. **API Specifications.** Arcadia shall provide complete, accurate API specifications and technical documentation for each EHR system to be integrated, no later than Week 6 of the implementation timeline (see Section 7.2). Late delivery of API specifications is the single most common cause of implementation delay in healthcare platform deployments and may result in corresponding delays to the Go-Live date.

4\. **Test Environments.** Arcadia shall provide access to EHR test environments, with representative data sets, for integration testing purposes. Test environments must be available no later than Week 10 of the implementation timeline.

5\. **Data Migration.** Arcadia shall be solely responsible for all data migration activities, including the retention and management of a qualified third-party systems integrator. Meridian strongly recommends that Arcadia engage its systems integrator no later than the commencement of Phase 2 (Week 5) to allow adequate time for coordination. Delays in data migration activities caused by Arcadia or its systems integrator shall not be attributable to Meridian.

6\. **Infrastructure Readiness.** Arcadia shall ensure that its network infrastructure, including bandwidth, firewall configurations, and VPN connectivity, meets the minimum technical requirements set forth in Appendix A prior to the commencement of Phase 3 (Week 13).

7\. **User Acceptance Testing.** Arcadia shall complete User Acceptance Testing within the timeframe specified in the final contract. Failure to complete UAT or to provide timely written notice of material nonconformity within the contractually specified period may result in deemed acceptance of the delivered system.

**9.2 Meridian Obligations**

1\. Meridian shall assign a dedicated Implementation Lead and project team with appropriate expertise in healthcare analytics deployments.

2\. Meridian shall deliver all in-scope implementation services in a professional and workmanlike manner consistent with industry standards.

3\. Meridian shall provide timely responses to Arcadia\'s technical inquiries and change requests.

4\. Meridian shall deliver software that performs substantially in accordance with the applicable Documentation for the warranty period specified in the final contract.

**9.3 Shared Assumptions**

1\. Both parties shall participate in weekly project status meetings throughout the implementation.

2\. The implementation timeline assumes no material changes to Arcadia\'s EHR environment or business requirements during the implementation period.

3\. All scope changes shall be documented through mutually executed Change Orders in accordance with the final contract\'s change management procedures.

4\. Force majeure events, including but not limited to natural disasters, pandemics, governmental actions, and telecommunications failures, may extend the implementation timeline without fault to either party.

**Minimum Client Infrastructure Requirements**

  Internet Connectivity               Dedicated, redundant internet connection with minimum 500 Mbps symmetrical bandwidth

**NexusCore Platform Specifications**

  Architecture                        Cloud-native, microservices-based, containerized (Kubernetes)

  Compliance Certifications           SOC 2 Type II, HITRUST CSF, HIPAA BAA available

  Availability SLA                    99.9% monthly uptime (excluding scheduled maintenance)

  Mobile Access                       Responsive web interface; native iOS and Android apps (read-only dashboards)
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[Appendix B --- Representative Client References]{.underline}**

Meridian is pleased to provide the following representative client references for Arcadia\'s due diligence. Contact information will be provided upon request, subject to client consent.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .xml and .rels part
        for name in names:
            if name.endswith(".xml") or name.ends

result:
EXHIBIT F

**From:** Rachel Okonkwo \<rokonkwo@arcadiahealth.com\> **To:** Sandra Bledsoe \<sbledsoe@meridiancloud.com\> **Date:** March 8, 2023, 2:47 PM CST **Subject:** NexusCore --- Performance Items for Your Team\'s Attention **CC:** Martin Schreiber \<mschreiber@arcadiahealth.com\>

Sandra,

I hope this note finds you well. Thank you again for your team\'s efforts in getting us through Go-Live in January --- I know the extended timeline was not easy on either side, and we appreciate the dedication your implementation group showed in the final push.

I am reaching out because our clinical informatics team has flagged a couple of items over the past few weeks that I wanted to bring to your attention so we can get them on your support team\'s radar.

First, we have been experiencing **intermittent latency issues** when running population-health queries against data sets exceeding roughly 500,000 patient records. The delays are inconsistent --- some queries return in the expected timeframe while others hang for upwards of four to five minutes on what should be comparable workloads. Our internal IT group has verified that our network throughput and cloud instance configurations are within the specifications outlined in the deployment guide, so we believe the issue may sit on the application side.

Second, several of our hospital analytics coordinators have reported **occasional report generation errors** in the scheduled overnight batch runs. Specifically, the pre-built readmission-risk reports are intermittently returning incomplete result sets or timing out entirely. We have not yet been able to identify a reliable pattern --- it seems to occur roughly two to three times per week across different facility nodes.

Neither of these issues has caused any patient-safety concerns, and our teams have been working around them, but I would love to get your support engineers looped in so we can troubleshoot before the volume picks up heading into Q2. If it would be helpful, Martin Schreiber on our side can coordinate access to our logging environment and schedule a joint diagnostic call at your team\'s convenience.

Please let me know the best way to get tickets opened for these items and whether you would prefer we route through the standard support portal or handle directly given the nature of the questions.

Looking forward to hearing from you.

Warm regards,

**Dr. Rachel Okonkwo** Managing Member & CEO Arcadia Health Systems, LLC 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210 San Antonio, TX 78229 (210) 555-4100 rokonkwo@arcadiahealth.com

**CHANGE ORDER NO. CO-004**

**AND STATEMENT OF WORK #1**

**Change Order Date:** December 5, 2022 **Change Order No.:** CO-004 **MSLSA Effective Date:** March 15, 2022 **SOW-1 Effective Date:** March 15, 2022

This Change Order No. CO-004 (\"**Change Order**\") is entered into as of December 5, 2022 (the \"**Change Order Effective Date**\"), by and between:

**Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.**, a Delaware corporation, with its principal place of business at 9200 Research Boulevard, Suite 400, Austin, TX 78759 (\"**Licensor**\" or \"**Meridian**\"),

and

**Arcadia Health Systems, LLC**, a Texas limited liability company, with its principal office at 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210, San Antonio, TX 78229 (\"**Licensee**\" or \"**Arcadia**\").

Licensor and Licensee are each referred to herein as a \"**Party**\" and collectively as the \"**Parties**.\"

This Change Order is issued pursuant to Section 5.4 (Change Orders) of the Master Software License and Services Agreement dated March 15, 2022, between the Parties (the \"**MSLSA**\"), and modifies the scope and fees of Statement of Work #1 executed concurrently therewith (\"**SOW-1**\"). Capitalized terms used but not defined herein shall have the meanings ascribed to them in the MSLSA or SOW-1, as applicable.

**1. Background and Purpose**

1.1 Under Section 3.2 of SOW-1, Licensee is solely responsible for all data migration activities, including extraction, transformation, and loading of legacy data from Licensee\'s existing HealthBridge 4.0 platform into the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module. Licensor\'s contractual obligation with respect to data migration is limited to advisory consulting services as described in Exhibit C to SOW-1.

1.2 In furtherance of its obligations under Section 3.2 of SOW-1, Licensee retained Linden Park Consulting, LLC, a Texas limited liability company (\"**Linden Park**\"), as its third-party systems integrator to design, develop, and execute data migration scripts and manage the extraction, transformation, and loading process.

1.3 During the extended implementation phase, Licensor\'s engineering team identified critical errors in data migration scripts prepared by Licensee\'s third-party systems integrator, Linden Park Consulting, LLC, which resulted in the following data integrity issues within the NexusCore staging environment:

> \(a\) Approximately 14,200 patient encounter records migrated with truncated or malformed diagnosis code fields, rendering the records unusable by the NexusCore analytics engine;
>
> \(b\) Duplicate record creation affecting approximately 8,700 unique provider identifiers due to faulty deduplication logic in Linden Park\'s ETL scripts;
>
> \(c\) Incorrect date-format mapping (MM/DD/YYYY vs. YYYY-MM-DD) across approximately 2.3 million transactional records migrated from HealthBridge 4.0, causing systemic errors in time-series reporting functions; and
>
> \(d\) Failure to map 37 legacy data fields identified in the Data Mapping Specification (Exhibit C-2 to SOW-1) to corresponding NexusCore schema fields, resulting in null-value population across multiple reporting dimensions.

1.4 Licensee has requested that Licensor perform data remediation services to identify, correct, and validate the errors described in Section 1.3 above, and Licensor has agreed to perform such services on the terms and conditions set forth in this Change Order.

**2. Scope of Data Remediation Services**

Licensor shall perform the following services (the \"**Data Remediation Services**\"):

> \(a\) **Diagnostic Assessment.** Comprehensive audit of all data migrated by Linden Park to identify the full extent of data integrity issues, including but not limited to those enumerated in Section 1.3.
>
> \(b\) **Script Remediation.** Development and execution of corrective transformation scripts to repair malformed, duplicated, and incorrectly mapped data records within the NexusCore staging and production environments.
>
> \(c\) **Data Validation.** Execution of automated and manual data validation protocols to verify the accuracy, completeness, and integrity of remediated data against source records extracted from HealthBridge 4.0.
>
> \(d\) **Reconciliation Reporting.** Delivery of a written reconciliation report documenting all corrections performed, records affected, and validation results.

**3. Fees.** The total fixed fee for the Data Remediation Services under this Change Order shall be **\$900,000.00** (nine hundred thousand dollars), payable as follows: fifty percent (50%) upon execution of this Change Order (\$450,000.00), and fifty percent (50%) upon Licensor\'s delivery of the reconciliation report described in Section 2(d) (\$450,000.00). All fees are subject to the payment terms of Section 6 of the MSLSA.

**4. Timeline.** Licensor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to complete the Data Remediation Services within forty-five (45) calendar days of the Change Order Effective Date.

**5. Acknowledgments.** 5.1 Licensee acknowledges and agrees that the data integrity issues described in Section 1.3 arose from errors in data migration scripts prepared and executed by Linden Park Consulting, LLC, and not from any defect in the NexusCore software or any act or omission of Licensor.

5.2 Nothing in this Change Order shall be construed as an assumption by Licensor of responsibility for data migration activities, which remain the sole responsibility of Licensee pursuant to Section 3.2 of SOW-1.

5.3 This Change Order does not modify, waive, or otherwise affect any limitation of liability, warranty disclaimer, or other provision of the MSLSA except as expressly set forth herein.

**6. General.** This Change Order is governed by and subject to the terms and conditions of the MSLSA. In the event of any conflict between this Change Order and the MSLSA, the MSLSA shall control except to the extent this Change Order expressly modifies the scope and fees of SOW-1. This Change Order may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original.

text:
All key facts are confirmed. The March 8 email is collaborative in tone (not a formal nonconformity notice) and came 22 days after the acceptance window closed. CO-004 — signed by Arcadia — expressly admits the data issues "arose from errors in data migration scripts prepared and executed by Linden Park... and not from any defect in the NexusCore software or any act or omission of Licensor." Both are referenced in/central to the FAC and usable on the motion.

Now I'll build the three documents. Let me write a Python script using python-docx for precise control over the court-formatting requirements (Times New Roman 12pt, double-spaced, 1" margins, centered page numbers, court caption).

tool_calls.name: write
tool_calls.arguments:
{"file_path": "build_docs.py", "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate motion-to-dismiss.docx, proposed-order.docx, cover-memo.docx
for Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.,
No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex. Austin Division).

Formatting per Judge Alvarez's Standing Order:
  - 12pt Times New Roman; double-spaced body; 1" margins; page numbers bottom-center.
"""
import re
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.enum.table import WD_TABLE_ALIGNMENT
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

FONT = "Times New Roman"
BODY_SIZE = 12
FOOTNOTE_SIZE = 10

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Low-level helpers
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def _set_run_font(run, size=BODY_SIZE, bold=False, italic=False, underline=False, font=FONT):
    run.font.name = font
    r = run._element
    rPr = r.get_or_add_rPr()
    rFonts = rPr.find(qn('w:rFonts'))
    if rFonts is None:
        rFonts = OxmlElement('w:rFonts')
        rPr.append(rFonts)
    for attr in ('w:ascii', 'w:hAnsi', 'w:cs'):
        rFonts.set(qn(attr), font)
    run.font.size = Pt(size)
    run.bold = bold
    run.italic = italic
    run.underline = underline


def _para_format(p, align=None, space_before=0, space_after=0, line=2.0, left_indent=None,
                 right_indent=None, first_line=None, keep_with_next=False):
    pf = p.paragraph_format
    if align is not None:
        p.alignment = align
    pf.space_before = Pt(space_before)
    pf.space_after = Pt(space_after)
    pf.line_spacing = line
    if left_indent is not None:
        pf.left_indent = Inches(left_indent)
    if right_indent is not None:
        pf.right_indent = Inches(right_indent)
    if first_line is not None:
        pf.first_line_indent = Inches(first_line)
    pf.keep_with_next = keep_with_next


def add_rich(p, text, size=BODY_SIZE, base_bold=False, base_italic=False):
    """Add runs to paragraph p, parsing **bold** and *italic* markers."""
    # Tokenize on ** and *
    tokens = re.split(r'(\*\*[^*]+\*\*|\*[^*]+\*)', text)
    for tok in tokens:
        if not tok:
            continue
        if tok.startswith('**') and tok.endswith('**'):
            run = p.add_run(tok[2:-1])
            _set_run_font(run, size=size, bold=True, italic=base_italic)
        elif tok.startswith('*') and tok.endswith('*') and len(tok) > 2:
            run = p.add_run(tok[1:-1])
            _set_run_font(run, size=size, bold=base_bold, italic=True)
        else:
            run = p.add_run(tok)
            _set_run_font(run, size=size, bold=base_bold, italic=base_italic)


def body_para(doc, text, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, first_line=0.5, space_after=0):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    _para_format(p, align=align, line=2.0, first_line=first_line, space_after=space_after)
    add_rich(p, text)
    return p


def flush_para(doc, text, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, space_after=0, bold=False):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    _para_format(p, align=align, line=2.0, first_line=None, space_after=space_after)
    add_rich(p, text, base_bold=bold)
    return p


def block_quote(doc, text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    _para_format(p, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, line=1.0,
                 left_indent=0.5, right_indent=0.5, space_before=6, space_after=6)
    add_rich(p, text)
    return p


def heading(doc, text, level=1, space_before=12, space_after=6):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    if level == 1:
        _para_format(p, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=2.0,
                     space_before=space_before, space_after=space_after, keep_with_next=True)
        add_rich(p, text, base_bold=True)
    else:
        _para_format(p, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=2.0,
                     space_before=space_before, space_after=space_after, keep_with_next=True)
        add_rich(p, text, base_bold=True, base_italic=(level == 3))
    return p


def center_line(doc, text, bold=False, size=BODY_SIZE, space_after=0, line=1.15):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    _para_format(p, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=line, space_after=space_after)
    add_rich(p, text, size=size, base_bold=bold)
    return p


def add_page_number_footer(section):
    footer = section.footer
    footer.is_linked_to_previous = False
    p = footer.paragraphs[0]
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
    _para_format(p, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=1.0, space_after=0)
    run = p.add_run()
    _set_run_font(run, size=BODY_SIZE)
    fld1 = OxmlElement('w:fldChar'); fld1.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'begin')
    instr = OxmlElement('w:instrText'); instr.set(qn('xml:space'), 'preserve'); instr.text = 'PAGE'
    fld2 = OxmlElement('w:fldChar'); fld2.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'end')
    run._r.append(fld1); run._r.append(instr); run._r.append(fld2)


def setup_document(doc):
    for s in doc.sections:
        s.top_margin = Inches(1); s.bottom_margin = Inches(1)
        s.left_margin = Inches(1); s.right_margin = Inches(1)
        add_page_number_footer(s)
    style = doc.styles['Normal']
    style.font.name = FONT
    style.font.size = Pt(BODY_SIZE)
    rpr = style.element.get_or_add_rPr()
    rfonts = rpr.find(qn('w:rFonts'))
    if rfonts is None:
        rfonts = OxmlElement('w:rFonts'); rpr.append(rfonts)
    for a in ('w:ascii', 'w:hAnsi', 'w:cs'):
        rfonts.set(qn(a), FONT)


def _no_borders(table):
    tbl = table._tbl
    tblPr = tbl.tblPr
    borders = OxmlElement('w:tblBorders')
    for edge in ('top', 'left', 'bottom', 'right', 'insideH', 'insideV'):
        e = OxmlElement(f'w:{edge}')
        e.set(qn('w:val'), 'nil')
        borders.append(e)
    tblPr.append(borders)


def caption(doc, title_text, response_deadline=None):
    """Standard W.D. Tex. caption with § brackets."""
    center_line(doc, "UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT", bold=True, space_after=0, line=1.15)
    center_line(doc, "WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS", bold=True, space_after=0, line=1.15)
    center_line(doc, "AUSTIN DIVISION", bold=True, space_after=6, line=1.15)

    rows = [
        ("ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,", ""),
        ("", ""),
        ("             Plaintiff,", ""),
        ("", ""),
        ("v.", "Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA"),
        ("", ""),
        ("MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,", ""),
        ("", ""),
        ("             Defendant.", ""),
    ]
    table = doc.add_table(rows=len(rows), cols=2)
    table.alignment = WD_TABLE_ALIGNMENT.LEFT
    table.autofit = False
    _no_borders(table)
    # column widths
    for r in table.rows:
        r.cells[0].width = Inches(4.2)
        r.cells[1].width = Inches(2.3)
    for i, (left, right) in enumerate(rows):
        c0 = table.rows[i].cells[0]
        c1 = table.rows[i].cells[1]
        p0 = c0.paragraphs[0]; _para_format(p0, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=1.15, space_after=0)
        if left:
            add_rich(p0, left)
        p1 = c1.paragraphs[0]; _para_format(p1, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=1.15, space_after=0)
        # § symbol on every row
        sec = p1.add_run("§  ")
        _set_run_font(sec, size=BODY_SIZE)
        if right:
            add_rich(p1, right, base_bold=True)
    # spacing after table
    sp = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(sp, line=1.0, space_after=2)

    # Document title
    center_line(doc, title_text, bold=True, space_after=2, line=1.15)
    if response_deadline:
        center_line(doc, response_deadline, bold=False, size=11, space_after=8, line=1.15)
    else:
        sp2 = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(sp2, line=1.0, space_after=4)
    # horizontal rule
    hr = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(hr, line=1.0, space_after=6)
    pPr = hr._p.get_or_add_pPr()
    pbdr = OxmlElement('w:pBdr')
    bottom = OxmlElement('w:bottom')
    bottom.set(qn('w:val'), 'single'); bottom.set(qn('w:sz'), '6')
    bottom.set(qn('w:space'), '1'); bottom.set(qn('w:color'), 'auto')
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)


def signature_block(doc, signers, date_line, firm_lines, party_line):
    sp = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(sp, line=1.0, space_after=6)
    p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=1.15, space_after=0)
    add_rich(p, "Respectfully submitted,")
    p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
    add_rich(p, firm_lines[0], base_bold=True)
    p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
    add_rich(p, "By: /s/ " + signers[0]['name'])
    for s in signers:
        p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
        add_rich(p, f"{s['name']}")
        p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
        add_rich(p, f"{s['bar']}")
    for fl in firm_lines[1:]:
        p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
        add_rich(p, fl)
    p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
    add_rich(p, party_line, base_italic=True)
    p = doc.add_paragraph(); _para_format(p, line=1.15, space_after=0)
    add_rich(p, date_line)

# ============================================================================
# DOCUMENT 1: MOTION TO DISMISS
